title: Platus
description: Platus is a composable private-account layer for Ethereum and EVM chains. Users deposit assets into a shielded pool of encrypted notes and transact through per-app stealth-address accounts, so existing dApps can recognise a returning user without learning their identity or linking activity across applications. Privacy rests on client-held viewing and spending keys with on-chain zk-SNARK verification, stealth addresses over Baby Jubjub, and hybrid post-quantum encryption.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Platus runs a UTXO-style shielded pool: the master account maintains the Merkle tree of all note commitments, each note committing Poseidon over asset, amount, owner, and nonce. The Action circuit lets a user privately spend existing notes and create new notes without revealing the notes themselves, so balances and amounts stay hidden in-pool. Value is conserved in-circuit: the sum of outputs is at most the sum of inputs, blocking inflation while keeping amounts private.

citations.cited_text: Commitment tree: Maintains the Merkle tree of all note commitments

All input and output values are range-checked

The sum of output values must be less than or equal to the sum of input values

Poseidon ​ 

Used for all in-circuit hashing (commitments, nullifiers).

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Platus hides both parties. It uses re-randomizable stealth addresses for unlinkable payments, where any third party can generate one-time addresses for a recipient without knowing their viewing key or linking addresses together. Senders spend notes through the shielded pool with zk-SNARK proofs of note ownership, Merkle membership, and nullifier correctness, hiding the spender. App accounts are deterministically derived per application, keeping activity unlinkable across different DApps.

citations.cited_text: Any third party can generate one-time addresses for a recipient without knowing their viewing key or linking addresses together.
Each app account is deterministically derived for a specific application, allowing protocols to maintain persistent state while keeping your activity completely unlinkable across different DApps.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: Notes encode the asset identifier as a hidden field of each note commitment, so observers cannot tell which token a shielded note represents. Commitments are computed as Poseidon over asset, amount, owner, and nonce and inserted into the Merkle tree, placing the asset inside the hidden preimage. The Action circuit then lets a user spend and create notes without revealing them, concealing the asset type during transfers. The asset is observable on-chain only at deposit and withdrawal.

citations.cited_text: Transfers assets from Deposit Manager to Master Account
Inserts commitments into the Merkle tree

Poseidon ​ 

Used for all in-circuit hashing (commitments, nullifiers).

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Entering Platus requires an explicit call to a protocol-specific contract: the user calls the Deposit Manager contract to instantiate a deposit request, an observable on-chain interaction with the privacy system. Funds move into the shielded pool when the Deposit Manager transfers them to the Master Account and a corresponding note is created, so the entry path is identifiably a Platus interaction. Opt-in shielded-pool entry is not indistinguishable from a plain public transfer.

citations.cited_text: The user calls the Deposit Manager contract to instantiate a deposit request.
Funds are transferred from the Deposit Manager to the Master Account and a corresponding note is created

properties.name: Time-to-finality
properties.notes: Platus is an application-layer protocol with no settlement step of its own — the bundler batches multiple operations into a single onchain transaction and submits the batched transaction to the Master Account, so transaction irreversibility follows the host EVM chain's finality.

citations.cited_text:
Step 4: Batching and Execution ​ 

The bundler collects multiple operations and batches them into a single onchain transaction and then submits the batched transaction to the Master Account.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: A user stores two independent secrets. The first is their pre-existing Ethereum wallet key, used to sign the L1 deposit transactions and pay gas. The second is the Platus spending key sk, the root secret from which all other keys are derived: the viewing key, identity key, ML-KEM key, hybrid key, and per-app keys all derive deterministically from sk. Because the shielded-side hierarchy roots in sk, only the wallet key and sk need independent storage.

citations.cited_text:
Spending Key (sk) ​ 

The spending key is the root secret from which all other keys are derived.
The viewing key is deterministically derived from spending public key
The identity key is derived from the viewing key a

properties.needsResearchReview: unconfirmed whether the Platus spending key derives from the EVM wallet signature or is independent

properties.name: Deposit time
properties.notes: Platus does not imposes a mandatory waiting period on deposits.

citations.cited_text: A deposit converts public ERC-20 into a single private note owned by a stealth address.

properties.name: Withdraw time
properties.notes: Withdrawing already-shielded notes is an ordinary on-chain transaction with no protocol-imposed delay, queue, or challenge window.

citations.cited_text: the client builds and proves, the bundler validates and aggregates, and the contract verifies and settles.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: While bundlers are permissionless actors that accept and batch operations, no current mechanism prevents a bundler from refusing to include a specific valid operation. To mitigate this, Platus allows multiple bundlers and user self-submissions.

citations.cited_text: Censorship: Mitigated by multiple bundlers, self-submission, rootHistory window

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: Platus relies on bundlers as an additional off-chain actor that batches user operations and posts them to the Master Account. Anyone can run a bundler, and bundlers accept operations from users, batch them, submit them on-chain, and get reimbursed for gas plus commission, so the relayer set is open-entry rather than curated. Bundlers add no separate staked consensus — they are reimbursed per-batch actors, but still an off-chain relayer layer beyond the host EVM chain.

citations.cited_text:
A bundler is a permissionless actor that:

Accepts operations from users

Batches them together

Submits them onchain

Gets reimbursed for gas costs + commission
Anyone can run a bundler.
The bundler collects multiple operations and batches them into a single onchain transaction and then submits the batched transaction to the Master Account.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: A user can reclaim a pending deposit at any time, instantly, before settlement completes: calling forceExit on the Deposit Manager contract returns the funds to the original deposit address with no waiting period. This covers funds still in the Deposit Manager only. For notes already shielded, the normal withdrawal path applies and no separate verifier-bypassing escape exists.

citations.cited_text:
How: 

Call the forceExit function on the Deposit Manager contract and the funds will be returned to your original deposit address.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: According to their technical docs and their manifesto, Platus is planning a inmutable protocol with no owner, no pause and no upgrades.
properties.needsResearchReview: Platus has not been deployed to mainnet or testnets so the upgradeability property is not verifiable.

citations.cited_text: No admin. There is no owner, no pause, no upgrade. The only privileged relationships are the onlyOrchestrator modifier on the Vault/AppAccount and the onlySelf modifier guarding executeAction

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.needsResearchReview: proving location inferred from bundler user-generates-proof wording, not stated on the circuits page
properties.notes: The user assembles the operation, which is itself a zk-SNARK proof that authorizes a transaction, then sends it to a chosen bundler. The circuits page lists the viewing key and spending key among the proof's private inputs. A user-assembled proof over a private witness held client-side is client-side proving, though the prover binary and SNARK backend are not described.

citations.cited_text: The user then sends this operation to their chosen bundler
a zk-SNARK proof that authorizes a transaction (e.g., transferring tokens, interacting with a DApp).

Private Inputs ​ 

Viewing key and spending key

Input note data (values, nonces, owners)

Merkle proofs for input notes

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: Proving for shielded transfers rests on a zk-SNARK Action and Merkle-membership circuit verified on-chain, with no external prover or validium exposing user witnesses to a third party. Recipients are hidden via re-randomizable stealth addresses, where any third party can generate one-time addresses without the viewing key, so observers without it cannot link activity. The off-chain risk engine sees only deposit source addresses, which is public chain data, not private state.

citations.cited_text: Any third party can generate one-time addresses for a recipient without knowing their viewing key or linking addresses together.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: Platus is pre-launch with no live deployment. As of December 2, 2025, a testnet launch is still weeks away and interested users are pointed to a waitlist rather than a live application. No contract addresses are published — the Contract Addresses page reads Coming Soon — and the product is described as a composable private account layer designed to normalize onchain privacy, still in its planning stage.

citations.cited_text: We're launching on testnet in the coming we

# Contract Addresses

_Coming Soon_
Platus is a composable private account layer designed to normalize onchain privacy.

properties.needsResearchReview: pre-launch, no deployed contract addresses or first-tx date to anchor a deployment date

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: Platus is not post-quantum secure for Harvest Now Decrypt Later (HNDL) attacks. The hybrid BJJ-KEM1024 construction (classical ECDH over Baby Jubjub plus ML-KEM-1024, FIPS 203) is scoped to note encryption only, so stored note contents stay confidential against a quantum adversary. Spend authorization uses classical Schnorr-style signatures over Baby Jubjub with no post-quantum proof system named but its future upgrade will not affect HNDL.

citations.cited_text: combining classical ECDH over Baby Jubjub with ML-KEM-1024 (FIPS 203) to provide post-quantum security for note encryption.
Notes remain secure if either ECDH or ML-KEM is unbroken. Both must fail for confidentiality to be compromised.
Schnorr-style signatures over Baby Jubjub with Poseidon challeng

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: There is no compliance enforcement in the current Platus implementation. It might not be in line with their Manifesto about permissionless on-chain privacy.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Only the user themselves can view their own transaction data. The dual-key architecture cryptographically isolates viewing from spending, with viewing keys derived for the user to scan stealth addresses generated by senders. User can share viewing keys with third parties such as auditors or regulators.

citations.cited_text: Platus implements a dual-key architecture where the ability to view transactions and the ability to spend funds are cryptographically isolated.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: The viewing key exists solely so the holder can scan the chain for notes addressed to them: stealth addresses let any third party generate unlinkable payment addresses without knowing the recipient's private keys, and the identity key derived from it cannot authorize spending or leak transaction history. No mechanism exists for granting, revoking, or updating viewing permissions to an auditor or counterparty, so there is nothing for the user to control along that axis.

citations.cited_text: a mechanism that allows any third party to generate unlinkable payment addresses for a recipient without knowing their private keys or previous transaction history.

Cannot authorize spending or leak transaction history

Used by third parties to generate stealth addresses

properties.name: Verifiability
properties.notes: Validity rests on re-checkable zk-SNARK proofs verified on-chain by the master account, which maintains the Merkle tree of note commitments. The Action circuit enforces note ownership, Merkle membership, nullifier correctness, value conservation where outputs sum to at most inputs, and a signature made with the spending key. Double-spend prevention is cryptographic: nullifiers are checked against prior spends and proofs verified through the Verifier contract, the bundle reverting on failure.

citations.cited_text:
Proof verification: Validates zk-SNARK proofs for operations

Operation execution: Processes user actions via App Accounts

All input and output values are range-checked

The sum of output values must be less than or equal to the sum of input values

Verify proofs through Verifier contract

If verification fails: Entire bundle reverts (no partial execution).

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: No public source repository, licence file, whitepaper, or audit is published for Platus. The Links page lists only external study materials on third-party primitives, not a code repository for the protocol itself. Without a published OSI-approved licence covering the contracts, circuits, SDK, or frontend, the protocol is closed source.

citations.cited_text: Study Materials

properties.needsResearchReview: no public repository, licence, or audit found for the protocol source

properties.name: Private State Scalability
properties.notes: The master account maintains the Merkle tree of all note commitments, an append-only structure that gains a leaf for every output note created. Each spend also publishes a nullifier derived from the note and viewing key, and nullifiers are exposed publicly so each note can only be spent once, so the nullifier set grows monotonically alongside the commitment tree. Both structures expand with transaction volume rather than with the number of distinct users.

citations.cited_text: Commitment tree: Maintains the Merkle tree of all note commitments

The provided nullifier is correctly derived from the note and viewing key

Nullifiers are exposed publicly, ensuring:

Each note can only be spent once

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: Recipients walk the chain's stealth-address outputs and, for each candidate pair (H1, H2), compute H2' = [vk]H1 with their viewing key and check whether H2' equals H2 to determine if the address belongs to them. This trial-check runs per candidate on the user's device, which is continuous client-side scanning. The recipient pulls (H1, H2) from the blockchain for each candidate, so no off-chain indexer is delegated the discovery task.

citations.cited_text:
H 2 ′ = [ v k ] H 1 H_2' = [vk]H_1 H 2 ′ ​ = [ v k ] H 1 ​ 

Checks if
H 2 ′ = H 2 H_2' = H_2 H 2 ′ ​ = H 2 ​ 

If equal, the address belongs to the recipient.
To check if a stealth address belongs to the user, the recipient:

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Platus manages private state as a note-commitment and nullifier system: deposits produce note commitments computed as Poseidon over asset, amount, owner, and nonce and inserted into a Merkle tree. Spending occurs through a Action circuit that lets a user privately spend and create notes without revealing them, with publicly exposed nullifiers ensuring each note can only be spent once. This commitment-tree plus nullifier design matches the UTXO-style shielded-pool pattern.

Inserts commitments into the Merkle tree

The provided nullifier is correctly derived from the note and viewing key

Nullifiers are exposed publicly, ensuring:

Each note can only be spent once

properties.name: Private Data Storage
properties.notes: Encrypted note ciphertexts live on-chain: notes are encrypted and stored onchain, and only the holder of the corresponding viewing key can decrypt and identify their notes. The master account holds note commitments in an on-chain Merkle tree, with deposits transformed into Poseidon-hashed note commitments (asset, amount, owner, nonce) inserted into the tree.

citations.cited_text: Notes are encrypted and stored onchain. Only someone with the corresponding viewing key can decrypt and identify notes belonging to them.
Transfers assets from Deposit Manager to Master Account
Inserts commitments into the Merkle tree

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.notes: Platus reaches external DeFi through per-application delegated accounts that wrap any unmodified Ethereum protocol, the adapter pattern: shielded value is spent into an app account, which calls the target protocol and routes proceeds back into a fresh note. Each app account is deterministically derived per application, keeping activity unlinkable across DApps. The worked flow swaps ETH for USDC on Uniswap via an app account. No whitelist constrains the target protocol.

citations.cited_text: App Accounts are application-specific delegated accounts that enable private, composable interactions with any protocol on Ethereum.
Each app account is deterministically derived for a specific application, allowing protocols to maintain persistent state while keeping your activity completely unlinkable across different DApps.

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Platus provides composable private interactions with arbitrary Ethereum protocols through per-application delegated accounts, going beyond pure payments but short of a general private VM. App accounts enable composable interactions with any protocol on Ethereum, each an isolated execution environment controlled by the master account via zk-SNARK proofs. The worked example is a private Uniswap swap through an app account, showing DeFi composability rather than user-defined private contracts.

App Accounts are application-specific delegated accounts that enable private, composable interactions with any protocol on Ethereum.
It creates an isolated execution environment controlled by your master account via zk-SNARK proofs.
